flashcards.back: A collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index.
O(1) - Constant time. Direct calculation of address using index.
O(n) - Must shift all n elements one position to the right.
Index represents offset from base address. First element is 0 positions away from start.

1. O(1) random access
2. Cache friendly (contiguous memory)
3. Memory efficient (no pointer overhead)

1. Fixed size (cannot grow)
2. O(n) insertion/deletion
3. Wasted space if not fully used

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Linear Search and Binary Search?
flashcards.back:
Linear Search: O(n), unsorted array, sequential search
Binary Search: O(log n), sorted array, divide-and-conquer approach

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'mid' calculation in Binary Search?
flashcards.back: To avoid integer overflow when calculating the midpoint of the search range.

flashcards.front: What is the tradeoff between Arrays and Linked Lists?
flashcards.back:
Arrays: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion
Linked Lists: O(1) insertion/deletion, O(n) access
